title: Mandarin and Scotch Bonnet Kosho Prawns
summary: These spicy prawns are super fresh and zingy. Make a big old platter of them and serve them  at your next pescatarian dinner party for a guaranteed good time.

method.description: On the day you want to cook the prawns, take the frozen mandarin juice and spicy chilli liquid out of the freezer first thing in the morning and leave on the counter to defrost.
Wash, pat dry and finely chop the coriander. Butterfly and devein the prawns. Drizzle with enough of the salty chilli liquid to coat them all over, plus the rapeseed oil.
In a bowl, mix together the kosho with the mandarin juice and half the chopped coriander.

Cook the prawns on a smoking hot griddle pan starting flesh-side down for 1-2 mins. Turn over and spoon the mandarin-chilli-herb mixture onto the prawn flesh, cooking for a further 1-2 mins until just opaque and cooked through.
Transfer the prawns to a large serving platter and sprinkle with the remaining coriander. Eat straight away.

chefs.name: Fatima Tarkleman
chefs.summary: Fatima “Fatti” Tarkleman is a proudly queer, migrant chef and recipe developer of mixed African-Asian descent. They have special interests in sustainability, fermentation, inclusivity, and local produce, while representing the flavours of their heritage and their London upbringing. Never conforming to societal norms, Fatti's cooking reflects their diverse background and refusal to be confined to traditional labels. As well as working at chic brunch spot Willows in south London, Fatti is the founder of queer dating supper club Eat Cute.  Rather than having a regional speciality, Fatti’s food is frequently inspired by special people in their personal life, and the loving meals they have shared. Headshot by Ken Lam
